_comment: Protocol-specific trivia questions for the Michigan base (mi_base) MCA set. Questions cover BLS protocols. 'min_level' indicates the minimum provider level this question applies to: MFR, EMT, AEMT, or Paramedic.

questions.question: Under WMRMCC protocols, which provider levels are authorized to administer a drawn-up epinephrine IM injection (from a vial) for anaphylaxis?
options: BLS (EMT) AND MFR — both require MCA-approved training
questions.explanation: WMRMCC approves drawn-up epinephrine IM for BLS (EMT) AND MFR scope — both require completion of MCA-approved training per Section 9-23R.

questions.question: Per WMRMCC protocols, what distinguishes anaphylaxis from a simple allergic reaction?
options: Anaphylaxis only occurs after bee stings
Anaphylaxis requires wheezing AND/OR hypotension — allergic reaction has only itching or hives
Anaphylaxis must include a rash covering more than 50% of the body
Any allergic response is automatically classified as anaphylaxis
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 1-6 distinguishes anaphylaxis (wheezing AND/OR hypotension) from allergic reaction (itching, hives without systemic involvement). Management differs significantly.

questions.question: For a pediatric patient weighing 10–30 kg presenting with anaphylaxis at WMRMCC, what is the correct epinephrine auto-injector dose?
options: Contact Medical Control — no dosing without online authorization
questions.explanation: WMRMCC protocols specify the pediatric auto-injector (0.15 mg IM) for children 10–30 kg. Contact Medical Control before administering to children appearing to weigh less than 10 kg.

questions.question: Under WMRMCC protocols, how many total doses of epinephrine (all sources combined) are permitted for anaphylaxis?
options: 2 doses maximum (all sources — auto-injector, EMS auto-injector, and draw-up combined)
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 1-6 specifies a maximum of 2 total epinephrine doses from all sources combined (prescribed auto-injector + EMS auto-injector + draw-up). The second dose may be given 3–5 minutes after the first if patient remains hypotensive.

questions.question: At WMRMCC, which provider can administer nebulized albuterol for wheezing in an anaphylaxis patient (Section B)?
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Scope Notes for Protocol 1-6, nebulized albuterol is EMT scope and is approved for BLS administration in both Section B (Anaphylaxis) and Section C (Allergic Reaction).

questions.question: Is diphenhydramine within BLS scope at WMRMCC?
options: Yes — EMTs can administer diphenhydramine IM for allergic reactions
Yes — MFR and above can administer it
No — diphenhydramine is ALS scope at WMRMCC
Yes — but only with online Medical Control authorization
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Scope Notes, diphenhydramine IM/IV is an ALS skill at WMRMCC. BLS providers cannot administer it independently.

questions.question: Per WMRMCC Protocol 1-6, can BLS administer the albuterol/ipratropium combination nebulizer for persistent wheezing in anaphylaxis?
options: No — albuterol/ipratropium combination is Paramedic only at WMRMCC
Yes — any BLS provider with training
questions.explanation: The albuterol/ipratropium combination is NOT approved for AEMT (Specialist) at WMRMCC — it is Paramedic only. AEMT option was not selected by WMRMCC.

questions.question: What is the epinephrine auto-injector IM scope at WMRMCC for treating anaphylaxis (EMS-administered, not patient's own)?
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Scope Notes, the EMS-administered epinephrine auto-injector IM is MFR scope at WMRMCC — EMT-Basic alone (without MFR designation) cannot perform this independently. However, BLS providers CAN perform draw-up epinephrine with MCA-approved training.

questions.question: Under WMRMCC Pediatric Seizure Protocol (4-7), what blood glucose level triggers treatment for hypoglycemia?
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-7 specifies treatment when blood glucose is less than 60 mg/dL. Oral glucose may be given if the patient is altered, able to swallow, and ≥3 months old.

questions.question: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-7, who can administer midazolam for an actively seizing pediatric patient?
options: Paramedic only — WMRMCC approved pre-radio for first dose
questions.explanation: Midazolam is Paramedic scope at WMRMCC. However, WMRMCC has approved pre-radio administration (no Medical Control contact required) for the first dose of midazolam in pediatric seizures.

questions.question: What is the IM dose of midazolam for a pediatric patient in active generalized tonic-clonic seizure, when IV access is not available?
options: 5 mg IM flat dose regardless of weight
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-7, midazolam for active tonic-clonic seizure (no IV): 0.1 mg/kg IM, maximum single dose 10 mg. If IV was established before seizure: 0.05 mg/kg IV/IO, max 5 mg.

questions.question: At WMRMCC, who can check blood glucose in a pediatric seizing patient?
options: Only with online Medical Control authorization
questions.explanation: Blood glucose testing is an MFR (and above) skill at WMRMCC per Protocol 4-7 scope notes.

questions.question: Per WMRMCC Pediatric Seizures Protocol, what should be done if seizures persist 10 minutes after the initial midazolam dose and blood glucose has been corrected?
options: Repeat midazolam one time — WMRMCC pre-radio approved for second dose
Contact Medical Control immediately before any further medication
questions.explanation: If seizures persist 10 minutes after initial midazolam AND glucose correction, repeat midazolam one time. WMRMCC has pre-radio approval for this second dose as well. If seizures persist after the second dose, contact Medical Control.

questions.question: Can a WMRMCC EMT assist a patient/caregiver in administering intranasal glucagon (Baqsimi) to a pediatric patient who has it prescribed?
options: No — glucagon is Paramedic scope only at WMRMCC
Yes — EMT may assist patient/caregiver if Baqsimi is prescribed to the patient, regardless of age
Only if the patient is under 2 years old
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-7 scope notes, EMTs may assist a patient or caregiver in administering prescribed Baqsimi (intranasal glucagon) regardless of age. Independently administered glucagon IM/IN by EMS is Paramedic scope at WMRMCC.

questions.question: Naloxone is within which scope for WMRMCC providers responding to a pediatric seizure with suspected opioid involvement?
options: BLS (MFR/EMT) scope per WMRMCC Opioid Overdose protocol
Requires Medical Control authorization for all levels
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-7 scope notes, naloxone is BLS scope per WMRMCC per the Opioid Overdose protocol. It can be administered by MFR/EMT providers.

questions.question: Under WMRMCC Pediatric Respiratory Distress Protocol (4-5), what is the SpO₂ target for pediatric respiratory patients?
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 specifies titrating oxygen to achieve SpO₂ of 94%. The protocol also notes that a parent can assist with blow-by O₂ or mask support for uncooperative children.

questions.question: What age range is croup most commonly seen in, per WMRMCC Protocol 4-5?
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 notes that croup is most common in children 6 months to 6 years of age and is commonly associated with recent upper respiratory infection or fever.

questions.question: At WMRMCC, which provider can administer racepinephrine 2.25% nebulized for a pediatric patient with stridor at rest (croup)?
questions.explanation: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-5, racepinephrine 2.25% nebulized (0.5 mL diluted in 3 mL NS) for croup is a Paramedic skill approved at WMRMCC.

questions.question: What is the correct dose of racepinephrine 2.25% for a pediatric patient with stridor at rest (croup) per WMRMCC Protocol 4-5?
options: 0.5 mL of racepinephrine 2.25% diluted in 3 mL normal saline, nebulized
2.5 mg in 3 mL NS (same as albuterol)
questions.explanation: Racepinephrine for croup: 0.5 mL of the 2.25% solution diluted with 3 mL normal saline, administered via nebulizer. Medical Control contact is not required per WMRMCC selection.

questions.question: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-5, in pediatric respiratory FAILURE (not just distress), what medication can a WMRMCC EMT administer?
options: Epinephrine draw-up IM or auto-injector IM — both EMT/MFR scope with required training
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 authorizes draw-up epinephrine IM and the auto-injector IM for pediatric respiratory FAILURE at EMT/MFR scope (training required). This is distinct from distress — failure indicates critical compromise.

questions.question: Is CPAP within EMT scope at WMRMCC for pediatric respiratory patients?
options: No — CPAP is AEMT scope only at WMRMCC
Yes — CPAP is EMT scope at WMRMCC, if appropriate size is available
CPAP requires Medical Control authorization for all levels
No — BVM only for pediatric patients
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 explicitly notes that CPAP is an EMT skill at WMRMCC, provided an appropriately sized mask is available and the patient can tolerate it.

questions.question: When using BVM ventilation on a child in respiratory arrest per WMRMCC Protocol 4-5, what is the preferred method for children under 8 years old?
options: Advanced airway (supraglottic or ET tube) is preferred
BVM with supplemental oxygen is the preferred method
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 specifies that BVM is the preferred ventilation method for children under 8 years old. Advanced airway is considered when unable to ventilate with BVM and basic adjuncts.

questions.question: What ventilation rate does WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 specify for an infant in respiratory failure?
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 specifies: Infant (approximately under 1 year) — 30 breaths/min; Child — 20 breaths/min when BVM ventilation is required.

questions.question: What clinical finding in a wheezing pediatric patient is described as 'critically reduced air movement' and an ominous sign in WMRMCC Protocol 4-5?
options: Mild retractions with wheeze on auscultation
Near-silent chest — loss of wheeze in a previously wheezing patient
questions.explanation: A near-silent chest in a wheezing patient indicates critically reduced air movement — so little air is flowing that wheeze cannot be generated. WMRMCC Protocol 4-5 describes this as a deterioration flag requiring immediate escalation.

questions.question: Per WMRMCC Pediatric Altered Mental Status Protocol (4-4), who can administer oral glucose to an altered pediatric patient with hypoglycemia?
options: EMT scope — if patient is altered, able to swallow, and ≥3 months old
MFR and above with Medical Control authorization
questions.explanation: Oral glucose is EMT scope at WMRMCC per Protocol 4-4. Requirements: patient must be altered, able to swallow, AND at least 3 months of age.

questions.question: Under WMRMCC Protocol 4-4, can BLS independently administer glucagon IM for a hypoglycemic pediatric patient who cannot receive IV dextrose?
options: Yes — glucagon IM is EMT scope at WMRMCC
No — glucagon IM is Paramedic scope at WMRMCC
questions.explanation: Glucagon IM is Paramedic scope at WMRMCC per Protocol 4-4. BLS providers cannot independently administer glucagon IM. EMTs may only assist with a patient's prescribed Baqsimi (intranasal glucagon).

questions.question: Per WMRMCC Protocol 4-4, when should naloxone be considered for a persistently altered pediatric patient?
options: Immediately for any altered pediatric patient
After glucose correction, if respiratory depression persists and patient has not regained consciousness despite adequate oxygenation/ventilation
Only if the patient is known to have used opioids
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-4 states: if respiratory depression persists AND the patient has not regained consciousness despite adequate oxygenation/ventilation support, administer naloxone per the Opioid Overdose protocol.

questions.question: What blood glucose threshold triggers treatment for hypoglycemia in WMRMCC Pediatric Altered Mental Status Protocol?
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-4 initiates hypoglycemia treatment when blood glucose is less than 60 mg/dL. This is consistent with Protocol 4-7 (Pediatric Seizures).

questions.question: In WMRMCC Protocol 4-4, what concentration of dextrose may be used for all pediatric age groups for IV dextrose administration?
options: D10% — 5 mL/kg (0.5 g/kg) up to 250 mL
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-4 states D10% may be used for all ages at 5 mL/kg (0.5 g/kg) up to 250 mL, always pushed slowly over 1–2 minutes. Higher concentrations require dilution and age-based calculations.

questions.question: What does WMRMCC Protocol 4-4 specify regarding newborns (less than 24 hours old) presenting with altered mental status?
options: Treat per standard Pediatric AMS protocol
Refer to the Newborn/Neonatal Assessment and Resuscitation-Treatment Protocol
Contact Medical Control before any interventions
questions.explanation: WMRMCC Protocol 4-4 explicitly states: for pediatric patients less than 24 hours old, refer to the Newborn/Neonatal Assessment and Resuscitation-Treatment Protocol rather than the standard Pediatric AMS protocol.
